title: TaskBoard vs Trello for Solo users
meta_description: TaskBoard vs Trello for solo users who want a kanban board that runs without server setup, updates, or ongoing hosting maintenance.
constraint_lens: You want a kanban board that runs without maintaining servers, hosting environments, or technical infrastructure.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for solo users who need a kanban board without maintaining infrastructure.
one_second_verdict.reason: TaskBoard fails first because self-hosted setup and server maintenance are required to keep the board running.

verdict.summary: Trello wins for solo users who want a kanban board without maintaining infrastructure. It runs as a hosted service where boards work immediately after creating an account. TaskBoard requires installing and hosting the application on a server or local environment. If self-hosted setup and server maintenance are required to keep the board running, TaskBoard fails first.
verdict.decision_rule: If self-hosted setup and server maintenance are required to keep the board running, TaskBoard fails first.

sections.heading: Best fit for maintenance-free kanban boards
sections.content: You host personal boards and refuse ongoing technical upkeep. Trello provides cloud-hosted boards that work immediately after account creation. TaskBoard must be installed on a server or local environment that you maintain.

bullets.point: Self-hosted kanban board running on your own server
bullets.why_it_matters: You control where the data lives instead of relying on a cloud service.

bullets.point: Lightweight PHP application with minimal interface
bullets.why_it_matters: The board runs with very little overhead once installed.

bullets.point: Local installation possible on private environments
bullets.why_it_matters: You can run the board entirely inside your own network.

bullets.point: Hosted kanban boards that run without server setup
bullets.why_it_matters: You create boards instantly without installing software.

bullets.point: Drag-and-drop card workflow in the browser
bullets.why_it_matters: You move tasks between stages without configuring infrastructure.

bullets.point: Automatic updates handled by the platform
bullets.why_it_matters: The board keeps working without manual upgrades or server management.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: You must install updates, manage hosting, or troubleshoot server issues to keep the board running.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Trello for a board that runs without infrastructure maintenance.

items.fails_when: You require full local control and cannot depend on cloud accounts or external hosting.
items.what_to_do_instead: Switch to TaskBoard if running the board locally is essential.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: If you already maintain a personal server and want full control over where board data is stored, TaskBoard may become practical.

sections.heading: Quick rules
rules: If you want a kanban board without server maintenance, choose Trello.
If hosting and updates sound like ongoing work, avoid TaskBoard.
If local hosting and full control matter more than convenience, consider TaskBoard.

faqs.a: Yes. It must be installed on a server or local environment that you maintain.

faqs.q: Is Trello hosted in the cloud?
faqs.a: Yes. Boards run on Trello servers and require no infrastructure setup.
Trello requires less maintenance because hosting and updates are handled automatically.

faqs.q: When would TaskBoard be the better option?
faqs.a: It is better when you need full local control and are comfortable running your own server.
